burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

career.notes: 'A man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous.' Donated $5,000 endowment for Yale's Dwight Professorship for Didactic Theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Endowed Yale's Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology
narrative_blocks.body: A New Haven hardware merchant remembered as 'a man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals.' He donated $5,000 to Yale to endow the Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology — named for his father Pope Dwight rather than himself.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Strong-Hooker line, fathered Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: In 1809 he married Clarissa Strong — daughter of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong, herself a Hooker descendant — bringing two independent Hooker descent lines into his children. Their son Timothy Dwight V became the 12th President of Yale 1886-1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant of liberal culture and fervid piety
narrative_blocks.body: Per the family memoir he was 'a man of liberal culture, of fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous; he believed in justice and right, and hated false shows and shams.' He had a 'fine intellectual face' and was 'of large frame and vigorous health' — a portrait of the merchant-evangelical type of his generation.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

career.notes: 'A man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous.' Donated $5,000 endowment for Yale's Dwight Professorship for Didactic Theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Endowed Yale's Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology
narrative_blocks.body: A New Haven hardware merchant remembered as 'a man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals.' He donated $5,000 to Yale to endow the Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology — named for his father Pope Dwight rather than himself.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Strong-Hooker line, fathered Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: In 1809 he married Clarissa Strong — daughter of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong, herself a Hooker descendant — bringing two independent Hooker descent lines into his children. Their son Timothy Dwight V became the 12th President of Yale 1886-1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant of liberal culture and fervid piety
narrative_blocks.body: Per the family memoir he was 'a man of liberal culture, of fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous; he believed in justice and right, and hated false shows and shams.' He had a 'fine intellectual face' and was 'of large frame and vigorous health' — a portrait of the merchant-evangelical type of his generation.

HD0371.prominent_name_reason: Through her marriage to Timothy Dwight Jr., Clarissa Strong connected Thomas Hooker's line to the Edwards-Dwight family. Her father-in-law Rev. Timothy Dwight Sr. was 8th President of Yale (1795-1817); his mother Mary Edwards was daughter of Jonathan Edwards; Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont descended from Thomas Hooker through Mary Hooker Pierpont → James Pierpont → Sarah Pierpont Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Massachusetts governor's daughter married a hardware merchant
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1809 at age 25 she married Timothy Dwight Jr. — hardware merchant, son of 8th Yale President 'Pope' Dwight — at her father's Northampton home. Her father had just completed seven Federalist terms as MA Governor; the marriage tied the Strong political dynasty to the Dwight intellectual one.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hooker descent for all seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Timothy Dwight Jr. was himself a Hooker descendant through his mother Mary Edwards Dwight, daughter of Esther Edwards. With her own Hooker descent through her mother Sarah Hooker, all seven of their children carried two independent lines of Thomas Hooker ancestry.

narrative_blocks.header: Her family’s papers fill a Yale archive
narrative_blocks.body: The Dwight family papers at Yale preserve the correspondence, sermons, and photographs of the clan she married into — Edwards, Hooker, Lyman, Strong, Woodbridge, and Woolsey relations all represented. She rests at Grove Street, steps from the college her father-in-law led.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her dead sister's husband, raised the sons
narrative_blocks.body: She married Dr. Jonathan Huntington Lyman Jr. in New Haven on 2 January 1855 — fourteen months after her sister Julia's tuberculosis death — and raised Julia's two surviving sons as her own. She bore no biological children and remained at Hadley MA for the marriage's 35 years until her 1886 death at 71, four years before Lyman's own.

sources.label: Homestead Museum blog — Dr. Jonathan Huntington Lyman
Thomas J. Farnham, Life, Adventures and Travels in California (1849)
Helen Hunt Jackson, A Century of Dishonor (1885) — Apache massacre letter citation

notable.primary_url_label: Homestead Museum — Lyman's Rowland and Workman account

education.school_name: University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine

narrative_blocks.header: Penn MD, California Gold Rush pioneer 1849
narrative_blocks.body: After earning his M.D. from the University of Pennsylvania he traveled to California as a 49er in the Gold Rush — leaving his new bride Julia Strong Dwight in New Haven only two years after their July 1847 wedding. He returned East after several years to resume practice in Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Tuberculosis took his wife at twenty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Strong Dwight bore him three sons between 1848 and 1853 — Edward Huntington (d. age 4), John Chester, and Francis Hinckley — but contracted tuberculosis and died in New Haven in December 1853 at age 29. Lyman was 37 and a widower with three children, the eldest just five.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his late wife's sister, 1855
narrative_blocks.body: On 2 January 1855 he married Mary Woolsey Dwight — Julia's older sister and two years his senior — in New Haven. She bore no children but raised Julia's surviving two sons; the marriage lasted 35 years until his June 1890 death at 73 in Hadley MA. Mary outlived him by 11 months.

narrative_blocks.header: Old Spanish Trail to California, 1841
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-five he joined the Rowland and Workman Expedition crossing from Santa Fe NM to Los Angeles via the Old Spanish Trail — a two-month overland journey through what he described as 'this valley of the Colorado of the West has no equal on this continent for barrenness... a great burial-place of former fertility.' The party arrived at LA on 5 November 1841.

narrative_blocks.header: Source for the foundational 1849 California book
narrative_blocks.body: His detailed account of the journey became the principal source material for Thomas J. Farnham's posthumous 1849 'Life, Adventures and Travels in California.' Subsequent California maps marked 'Dr. Lyman's route' — though Farnham wrote that he feared his friend would never be persuaded to write his own book, denying him the place in border literature he deserved.

narrative_blocks.header: Apache massacre letter cited by Helen Hunt Jackson
narrative_blocks.body: In a letter published in an 1871 federal Indian report he related an account of an American massacre of Apaches in the Mexican state of Sonora during his 1840-1841 travels. Helen Hunt Jackson cited Lyman's letter in her 1885 indictment of US Indian policy 'A Century of Dishonor.'

narrative_blocks.header: Northampton mansion is now Smith's Sessions House
narrative_blocks.body: In 1859 he bought the lot across Elm Street from his existing house from Samuel Hopkins and had William Fenno Pratt design and build a new house in 1860. Sold for $13,000 to Orleana Boker in 1866; the property is now Sessions House at Smith College Northampton, still standing 165 years later.

career.role: Pioneer traveler with the Rowland and Workman Expedition
career.location: Santa Fe NM → Los Angeles via Old Spanish Trail
career.notes: Traveled with John Rowland, William Workman, Benjamin D. Wilson, Albert Toomes, Wade Hampton, William Gambel, Isaac L. Given and others; arrived LA Nov 5 1841. Spent ~18 months in CA recovering at Don Abel Stearns' home. Returned East 1843 by Wilson's account (some sources 1845). His account preserved in Farnham 1849 book.

Practiced medicine for ~15 years; by 1860 census self-reported as 'gentleman' with $22,000 wealth. By 1870 census listed as attorney with $40K real estate + $250K personal property = $290K total — dramatic wealth jump possibly from law practice, inheritance, or northwestern railroad investments mentioned in obituaries.
Returned to CA during Gold Rush, leaving wife Julia in New Haven 2 yrs after their wedding. Brief return engagement with the frontier.

Moved to SF with 2nd wife Mary + son John in mid-1870s. Sold Northampton house (now Sessions House Smith College). Listed San Rafael 1879 voter reg as 'retired physician.' Sold son John some property in greater LA area 1879. Cathedral Hill SF 1882. Returned to Northampton after Mary's death mid-1880s.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.
A Strong cousin named his son Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Sophia Dwight Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: His bride was also his cousin, Sophia Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight married Sophia Dwight in June 1845 — his first cousin, daughter of Dr. Benjamin Woolsey and Sophia (Strong) Dwight of Clinton, New York. Both were grandchildren of President Timothy Dwight of Yale; cousin marriage was legal and common in New England.

narrative_blocks.header: His saltpeter business fed the gunpowder trade
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight established himself in New Haven as a manufacturer of saltpetre — potassium nitrate, the primary oxidizer in gunpowder. He operated through the 1840s and into the Civil War decade, when demand for gunpowder ingredients reached its wartime peak.

career.location: New Haven, New Haven County, Connecticut
career.notes: Manufacturer of saltpetre (potassium nitrate) at New Haven CT. Per EH 1909 #2681.

cross_connections.notes: HD1990 Sophia Dwight married her first cousin HD1982 John William Dwight, Jun 18, 1845.
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

cross_connections.notes: HD1990 Sophia Dwight married her first cousin HD1982 John William Dwight, Jun 18, 1845.
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband John William Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage to another Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 June 1845 — exactly the day she would die eighteen years later — she married her first cousin John William Dwight, son of her uncle Timothy Dwight Jr. and Clarissa Strong of New Haven. The marriage doubled her children's Hooker descent through two paths back to Pope Dwight and gave them the unusual 'Dwight Dwight' surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children, three lost before age four
narrative_blocks.body: She bore six children between 1849 and 1860: Mary Clare lived to 91; Dr. Edward Strong became a Massachusetts physician living to 87; and Emily Cecelia reached 30. Three sons died young — Henry Woolsey at 1, Frederic Louis at 3, and Louis Theodore at 2. She herself died at 45 in New Haven on her own 18th wedding anniversary.

narrative_blocks.header: Born May 1847; dead in Clinton, three months
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia Clarissa Dwight was born May 1, 1847 at New Haven and died August 21, 1847 in Clinton, New York — three and a half months old. She was the first of John William and Sophia Dwight's seven children.

cross_connections.display_label: Father-in-law: Union chaplain who served Bull Run to Appomattox

institutions.notes: Andover Theological Seminary after Yale 1838; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME parish.

burial.plot_notes: Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA — with 2nd wife Elizabeth McGregor Adams Dwight + 2 of their daughters

narrative_blocks.header: Yale theologian and Gorham Maine pastor
narrative_blocks.body: Born 30 April 1820 New Haven CT to the hardware merchant Timothy Dwight Jr and his MA-Governor's-daughter wife Clarissa Strong. Yale BA 1838 followed by Andover Theological Seminary; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME — where he met his first wife and later his second.

narrative_blocks.header: Third sister-replacement marriage isn't this one
narrative_blocks.body: His own pattern was DIFFERENT from the Lyman + Anthony sister-replacement marriages in his family. His first wife Lucy Waterman died at 38 in 1861; three years later in 1864 he married not her sister but his parish's Sunday-school superintendent's daughter — Elizabeth McGregor Adams, child of his Gorham predecessor Rev. John Ripley Adams.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a Civil War chaplain's family, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1864 — while his future father-in-law was serving as chaplain of the 121st New York Volunteer Infantry in the Army of the Potomac — he married Elizabeth McGregor Adams at her Gorham ME family home at age 44. Her father Rev. John Ripley Adams would survive Appomattox by one year then die of brain inflammation caused by war stress.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both wives and one son
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Waterman 1861 at 38; Elizabeth Adams 1879 Independence Day at 42; son Edward Huntington 1893 at 37 — three premature deaths in his immediate family before his own 1890 death at Hadley MA at age 70. His two younger daughters by the second marriage lived to 88 and 90.

career.notes: Settled in Gorham ME after Yale + Andover; met both wives there.

burial.plot_notes: Eastern Cemetery, Gorham ME — separate plot from her husband + 2nd wife who are buried at Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Dwight Yale theologian at twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: In October 1849 at age twenty-six she married Rev. Edward Strong Dwight — Pope Dwight's grandson and a recent Andover Theology graduate — at her hometown Gorham ME. They had two children: Annie Waterman (1851) and Edward Huntington (1856) born in Maine.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-eight, buried in Maine
narrative_blocks.body: She died in Gorham ME on 11 September 1861 at thirty-eight — the first year of the Civil War. Her husband remarried less than three years later to Elizabeth McGregor Adams of Gorham (daughter of his parish's former pastor turned Union chaplain). She was buried at Gorham's Eastern Cemetery, not at Phillips Academy where her successor wife and children would lie.

burial.plot_notes: Phillips Academy Cemetery, Andover MA — with husband Rev. Edward Strong Dwight and their 2 daughters

parents.mother_name: Mary Anne McGregor Adams of Londonderry NH

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War chaplain's daughter, married the widower
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1 October 1836 in Gorham ME, she was the daughter of the Congregational pastor Rev. John Ripley Adams — who in 1861 became Union chaplain of the 5th Maine through the entire Civil War. In July 1864 at age 27, while her father was still in the field, she married her father's parishioner Rev. Edward Strong Dwight, widower of Lucy Waterman who had died at Gorham three years earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Died Independence Day 1879 at forty-two
narrative_blocks.body: She died at Hadley MA on Independence Day (4 July) 1879 at age forty-two, leaving two daughters Marion McGregor (13) and Julia Strong Lyman (8). Both daughters lived to extraordinary longevity — Marion to 88 in 1954 and Julia to 90 in 1961 — though their mother and grandfather had both died at or before their early sixties from war-related stresses.

narrative_blocks.header: Oldest child of Rev. Edward Strong Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1851 Gorham ME to Rev. Edward Strong Dwight and his first wife Lucy Waterman, she was their elder of two children. Her mother died when she was 10 (1861); raised through teen years by her father then her stepmother Elizabeth McGregor Adams. Marriage and later life details pending research.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-seven, three years after father
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1856 Gorham ME to Rev. Edward Strong Dwight and Lucy Waterman, he was five when his mother died at Gorham. He died at thirty-seven in 1893 — three years after his father's 1890 death — extending the Dwight family pattern of premature deaths in the Timothy Jr branch.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived eighty-eight years, Civil War to Cold War
narrative_blocks.body: Born December 1865 — seven months after Lee's surrender at Appomattox where her grandfather Rev. John Ripley Adams had served as Union chaplain — she was the elder daughter of Rev. Edward Strong Dwight and Elizabeth McGregor Adams. She lost her mother at 13 (1879) and lived 88 years until April 1954, the Cold War's beginning. Buried Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety years, named for two aunts
narrative_blocks.body: Born December 1870 Hadley MA — named for her two aunts Julia Strong Dwight Lyman (tuberculosis 1853 age 29) and Mary Woolsey Dwight Lyman (1886 age 71). She lost her mother at 8 on Independence Day 1879 and lived 90 years through two world wars to die in July 1961 at Brookline MA. Buried Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Jamaica, leaving three children under six
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Strong Dwight died December 4, 1853 in Jamaica, West Indies at twenty-nine — her youngest child Francis had just been born in Chicago months before, and the oldest was only five years old. Within thirteen months of her death, her widower Dr. Jonathan Huntington Lyman married her sister Mary Woolsey Dwight, keeping the children within the Dwight family.

narrative_blocks.header: First Lyman son lost at age four
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1848 as the first child of Julia Strong Dwight and Dr. Jonathan H. Lyman Jr., he died at approximately four years old in 1852 — a year before his mother's tuberculosis death. He was buried in Northampton MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Last surviving descendant of Yale's Pope Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Born May 1851 in New Haven, he was the middle son of Julia Dwight + Dr. Lyman. Per the family memoir he was the 'son and last surviving descendant' of his line back to 8th Yale President Pope Dwight. He died at Chicago in March 1904 at 52 and was buried at Bridge Street Cemetery Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard Medical 1872, California physician
narrative_blocks.body: After two years at Yale Scientific Department he graduated Harvard Medical School in 1872 — the year his father's wealth peaked at $290,000. He followed his father to California in the mid-1870s and practiced medicine in San Francisco and across the bay at San Rafael for roughly thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Valvular heart disease in Chicago, 1904
narrative_blocks.body: He died at the German-American Hospital in Chicago on 29 March 1904 at age 52 — after a six-month illness of valvular heart disease. He was buried at Bridge Street Cemetery Northampton MA with his parents and brothers — the last of the Lyman family's New Haven generation to die.

burial.plot_notes: Bridge Street Cemetery, Northampton MA — with parents Jonathan H. Lyman Jr. + Julia Dwight Lyman

career.location: San Francisco then San Rafael CA
career.notes: Followed father to California in mid-1870s. Practiced in SF and San Rafael. Died of valvular heart disease at Chicago German-American Hospital after 6-month illness.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of yellow fever in Brazil at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Francis Hinckley Lyman was born January 19, 1853 in Chicago while his family was traveling — his mother Julia died in Jamaica the same year. He died July 20, 1871 of yellow fever in Brazil at eighteen: both he and his mother died young in tropical places, each of them thousands of miles from the New Haven family home.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven of Timothy Jr's, died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1825 New Haven to Timothy Dwight Jr and Clarissa Strong, she was one of seven siblings carrying double Hooker descent. She died in 1864 at age 39 — the same year her cousin Mary Dwight Anthony died in Chicago, also age 39 — compounding family losses in the war year.

narrative_blocks.header: Last of Timothy Jr's children, died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1831 to Timothy Dwight Jr and Clarissa Strong as their seventh child, she died in 1870 at age 39. With sister Julia (TB 1853 age 29), brother John William's wife Sophia Dwight (1863 age 45), and sister Mary (1864 age 39), she completed a generation of premature deaths in the Timothy Jr branch.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved to Chicago; made paper bags
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight moved from New Haven to Chicago and built a paper bag manufacturing business — an industry rapidly growing mid-century. His son Timothy worked alongside him as a civil engineer.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of Yale's president; built Chicago business
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight was born in New Haven in 1843 — great-grandson of Yale President Timothy Dwight — and moved to Chicago where he worked in his father's paper bag business as a civil engineer. He married Delia Allen Williams on December 15, 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at four years old in 1849
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Cecil Dwight was born April 9, 1845 and died May 19, 1849 at four years old. He was the second of Timothy and Lucy Olmstead Dwight's seven children, and one of three who died in early childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eighteen months old, 1848
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Thomas Dwight was born January 18, 1847 and died July 31, 1848 at about eighteen months old — the second of two infants to die within three years in this family.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at three years old in 1852
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Frederic Dwight was born April 12, 1849 and died July 31, 1852 at three years old. Three of Timothy Dwight's seven children died before age five — Edward Cecil, Henry Thomas, and Charles Frederic.

narrative_blocks.header: Born October 24; dead four days later
narrative_blocks.body: Francis Alfred Dwight was born October 24, 1853 and died October 28, 1853 — four days old. Of Timothy and Lucy Olmstead Dwight's seven children, four died before age five.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the Governor; died at two
narrative_blocks.body: Caleb Strong Dwight was born September 1812, named for his grandfather Governor Caleb Strong of Massachusetts. He died July 7, 1814 at about two years old.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died 1816; first named Edward Strong
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong Dwight was born in 1816 and died the same year, named for his uncle Edward Strong who had died at twenty-two after Harvard. His parents later gave the same name to a second son.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah Hooker; died at fifteen years old
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Dwight was born July 20, 1822 in New Haven, carrying the name of her great-grandmother Sarah Hooker Strong — the third generation of women in this family to be named Sarah Hooker. She died May 8, 1838 at fifteen years old.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Sarah Hooker after great-grandmother H00525 — third generation to carry this name alongside H02675 and H04225.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother; died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Dwight was born September 22, 1826 and died January 1828 at fifteen months, named for her mother Clarissa Strong Dwight. Of their nine children, four died before adulthood.

cross_connections.notes: No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.
No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.

cross_connections.display_label: Called Yale colleague James Luce Kingsley 'the American Addison'

cross_connections.description: Talcott-side descendant given middle name 'Dwight' in 1855, likely after Pope Dwight (8th Yale president); a second Timothy Dwight Hobart (TD0131 b.1826) carries the same name pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: He made Yale the nation’s largest college
narrative_blocks.body: As Yale’s eighth president from 1795 to 1817 he hired Benjamin Silliman as its first chemistry professor — later called the patriarch of American science — and grew Yale into the largest college in the country. His revival sermons there helped touch off the Second Great Awakening.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for independence a year early
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1775, a full year before the Declaration, he was openly urging the colonies to declare independence — by his own account using the very arguments later adopted — while many around him were still fearful or opposed.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote America’s first epic poem
narrative_blocks.body: In 1785 he published “The Conquest of Canaan,” an eleven-book biblical epic dedicated to George Washington and reckoned the first epic poem written in America. He was a leading “Hartford Wit” alongside John Trumbull, Joel Barlow, and Noah Webster.

narrative_blocks.header: A mission in Arkansas still bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: He helped found the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in 1810; a decade later, in 1820, its Dwight Mission to the Cherokee in Arkansas was named in his honor. As a Continental Army chaplain in the Revolution he had written the patriotic song “Columbia” for his troops.

narrative_blocks.header: Stolen pears taught him the eighth commandment
narrative_blocks.body: As a small boy he took pears from a neighbor’s yard with older schoolmates; his mother told him it broke the commandment against stealing. He returned them in tears and refused to keep them even when the owner offered — a story he later preached to his Yale congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote a war anthem as army chaplain
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as a chaplain in the Continental Army, Timothy Dwight wrote the patriotic song "Columbia." Set to music, it became one of the young republic's stirring anthems.

artworks.blurb: John Trumbull oil portrait, painted 1817 — year of Pope Dwight's death.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding figure; Dwight Mission named in his honor.

institutions.blurb: Taught at Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven after his 1769 Yale graduation, before becoming a Yale Tutor.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Mary Woolsey, son James Dwight III, granddaughter Aurelia, grandson Tim V

education.notes: Entered Yale at age 13; graduated top of

career.notes: pastor; ran a noted boys’ academy

parents.father_name: Benjamin Woolsey of Long Island NY — was Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend
parents.father_research_notes: Mary's father Benjamin Woolsey predeceased the Dwight marriage. Earlier tree data incorrectly set father_id=H00914 (her HUSBAND Pope Dwight). Corrected Stage 6Y-161.

bio.extra_names: Sometimes Margaret (EH 914 calls her Margaret; FAG and consensus sources Mary; per Sam, the same person — Mary as nickname for Margaret)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Pope Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Brother and sister-in-law cemented Woolsey-Dwight ties
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Woolsey had been Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend — the Woolsey-Dwight connection preceded her own 1777 marriage to Pope Dwight. Her brother William Walter Woolsey later married Elizabeth Dwight, sister of her husband Pope Dwight, doubling the family link in the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Unknown grave near Natchez, Adams County, Mississippi — died in the wilderness 1777, body location never recorded

career.notes: Merchant in Northampton MA. Substantial family business.
Judge 1758-1774. Took oath of fealty to British crown on assuming office — held this oath binding through the Revolution and refused to revoke it.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist judge who would not break his oath
narrative_blocks.body: He had sworn fealty to the British crown in 1758 on becoming judge of the court of common pleas, and held that oath binding when the Revolution came. His Northampton family suffered increasing community ostracism as the war approached; one biographer called him 'a Loyalist on Christian principle, and yet thoroughly patriotic in his feelings.'

narrative_blocks.header: Took two sons to a Mississippi colony, 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with revolutionary Northampton, in May 1776 he sailed from Middletown CT with two of his sons — Sereno and Jonathan — toward a British land grant at Natchez Mississippi. They were accompanied by the Phelps and Lyman families and the Rev Mr Smith's household; within a year he and Mrs Lyman would both be dead at Natchez of fever.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in the wilderness, body never recovered
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Natchez Mississippi in June 1777 at age fifty in the wilderness, without trial or recorded grave. News of his death traveled slowly back to Northampton; his son Timothy Dwight IV left the Continental Army in 1778 to return home as the oldest surviving male and take charge of the family's affairs.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried tiny wife on his palm, arm's length
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Edwards Dwight was as small as he was large — he sometimes carried her around the room on his open palm held out at arm's length. She bore him thirteen children, eight of them sons, all noted for fine physical forms — the smallest son Cecil was five-foot-eight-and-a-half and weighed two hundred pounds.

cross_connections.display_label: Refused his Northampton congregation, took communion twelve miles away instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen as Edwards left for Stockbridge
narrative_blocks.body: Her November 1750 wedding to Major Timothy Dwight came two months before Jonathan Edwards moved his family to the Stockbridge mission. The marriage was permitted because the family was about to be separated.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale's eighth president Timothy Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Timothy Dwight IV led Yale College from 1795 to 1817. He shaped a generation of New England intellectual life from his grandfather Edwards's old territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Refused her pew when Hooker succeeded her father
narrative_blocks.body: When the distant Thomas Hooker descendant Rev. John Hooker became Northampton's pastor after Edwards's dismissal, she placed her own chair in the vestibule below the belfry. On communion Sabbaths she rode twelve miles on a pillion behind her son to take communion at another church.

narrative_blocks.header: Heard a year later her husband had died
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Dwight III died at Natchez, Mississippi in 1777 during a southern trip. The news did not reach her at Northampton until a full year after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband could carry her on his open palm
narrative_blocks.body: She was unusually small in stature and was described in old age as 'opinionated and self-willed.' Patriot rabble burned her fields and stole her livestock during the Revolution because of her husband's Tory sympathies.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
